text_clean: An unexpectedly fascinating, detailed, and informative museum about a culture which is little-known outside of Peru. The exhibition of artefacts along with explanation of the excavation process and sections about the issue of looting of archaeological sites in Peru works really well. The entrance fee is also very reasonable.

text_clean: It might sound insane to rate this site as 2 star consider is a world heritage site but this was one of the most deflating experiences of my trip. We got the circuito 1 ticket which basically keeps you out of the site and you get is a view. AND NOTICE ONCE UP THERE YOU CANNOT BUY A SECOND TICKET . When we realized this we tried to get another ticket but we were told we needed to go down to the town and buy another one and then go back up again. Which means a couple of hours by bus and your train tickets might be lost as they also have set times. To be honest they should not sell this ticket! thank God they sun came out and we had a site other wise we would have spend $300 to see a cloud! I really dislike how artificial and turistic this attraction is and how circuito 1 feels more like a scam

Which means a couple of hours by bus and your train tickets might be lost as they also have set times. To be honest they should not sell this ticket! thank God they sun came out and we had a site other wise we would have spend $300 to see a cloud! I really dislike how artificial and turistic this attraction is and how circuito 1 feels more like a scam

text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

text_clean: It was nice, not even close to my favorite thing in Peru or my favorite trek but I would say definitely worth two to three days if you have time and are in the arequipa area. It is a very developed area for tourism so expect it to be busy and to have to pay for camping. I was recommended to take 5 litres of water, I took 6, and I did drink around 4, the reason I am included this however is that there were multiple locations where I could have refilled with my filter but didn't need to, therefore unless you drink a very large amount of water I wouldn't bring 6 litres. This is of course in the part of the canyon I did which started near cabanaconde.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo en cuanto a objetos y diseño es un 10/10. PERO llegué 430pm, 1era vez que visito el museo, cuando me vendieron las entradas, nunca me dijeron q la hora de cierre estaba próxima. Después cuando estaba dentro disfrutando del museo, alguien del museo me apuró, me dijo "en 1 hr se ve todo, ajustando 45min". Ir a un museo es una experiencia, sobre todo un museo con tantos detalles como este. No pude leer ni admirar el 50% de los objetos. Cada objeto tiene un texto bastante detallado además de ser obras muy bien trabajadas, obras q uno necesita procesar con tiempo, admirar. Soy de Lima, no sé cuando regrese, lamentablemente no tuve una buena experiencia. destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: In September 1926 , Professor Julio C.Tello and his team of archaeologists were digging in the heart of the Nazca Valley in Peru . One day , two of the team members went for a walk . They wandered off in the direction of a hill . When they reached the top , they looked down and noticed something truly spectacular . The desert below was full of huge drawings of various geometric shapes . Apar from those there were also drawings of animals , birds , insects . Some were about two hundred meters long . Scientific testing confirmed that the drawings were more than two thousand years old . Scientists have many theories about why the ancient Nazca people made these lines . However ,for the time being , there is no definite answer . Some believe that the Nazca people knew how to fly and could see the drawings from the sky . Others believe they were a special calendar for the stars . Nowadays ,the Nazca lines are one of Peru 's most famous landmarks and if you want to experience them for yourself , you can go on a guided tour .

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The content of the museum is amazing and some of the best I've seen in South America. Unfortunately the museum is massively let down by the lack of story and content conveyed by the information boards. Also none of the information on the second and third, the lowest level, has any translation into other languages and the top level only into English (about 25% of the whole museum) so its pointless going if you want to learn and not just see pretty objects. I believe they have the option of a guide to squeeze more money from you if you like. I'd recommend reading up beforehand so you know what your going to see.

text_clean: Interesante lugar aunque el recorrido es corto. Tiene un restaurante acogedor con mira hacia la huaca. Lamentablemente ese día nos tocó una guía que nos trató mal debido a que demoramos unos minutos en unirnos a un grupo porque esperabamos a dos amigos más que estaban en camino. Cabe resaltar que la mitad del grupo (y los que estaban en camino) eran turistas-estudiantes extranjeros. Como no había otra guía, nos tocó ir con ella. text_clean: A spectacular canyon, and in my view even more spectacular historical site. The terraces built by the pre-inca civilitation that stretch through the valley are stunning. I cannot begin to imagine the amount of labour and sheer effort that went into building these. There are some excellent opportunities to take short hikes to scenic viewpoints, and inbetween you get around easily by bus or car, so it's very well suited for the not so outdoor-minded among us, which includes me. There is also an active volcanoe that erupts from time to time, making for a spectacular view.

text_clean: El día de ayer 08/02/2026 fuimos con la familia al circuito mágico de las aguas, y realmente deja mucho que desear el trato de las personas que trabajan ahí… no hay asientos suficientes para sentarse. Estás parado en toda la calor horas de horas no les importa no te dejan pisar las áreas verdes ni para sentarte un momento pero sin embargo hay juegos de niños que cobran y están ubicados en áreas verdes y ahí si se puede pisar qué ilógico qué mala logística que maltrato a la gente. Sumado a eso todo es caro adentro. Y en los juegos de agua te dejan ingresar solamente 5 minutos nada mas… pesimo 👎🏻 Fue una mala experiencia realmente. 👎🏻👎🏻 Gracias

text_clean: I know, I know.. you’d think I’d give a place like Machu Picchu more than 3 stars… but honestly I’ve been here twice and, while definitely dramatic looking, unless you seriously love Incan culture or are willing to pay for a guide, you won’t get much out of the experience other than Instagram content… Speaking of which, if you want to avoid getting a million people in your photos you have GOT to pick the first slots of the morning (06:00a), I can’t stress this enough… don’t go after 10am unless you lovvvvvve huge crowds, heat, and long queues. The Peruvian government is keeping some of the silly covid changes in place, namely the unidirectional walking path and new “circuits” method of ticketing where they’ve split the park up and you have to purchase multiple tickets to see the whole thing, an obvious money grab at this point and another reason I knocked off a star.

text_clean: Arequipa is an incredible experience overall and surprisingly affordable! Known as the White City, Arequipa boasts charming architecture and a vibrant culture that makes every visit special. One of the highlights of my trip was visiting the breathtaking Colca Canyon. The landscapes of Colca Canyon are absolutely unbelievable. The vast, rugged beauty of the canyon leaves you in awe, and it's a fantastic place to connect with nature. The tranquility and peacefulness you find there are unmatched. It's a perfect escape from the hustle and bustle of daily life. Walking along the canyon, you can truly appreciate the serene environment and the stunning views that stretch as far as the eye can see. text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

text_clean: Una exposición muy bien montada y organizada que no solo contempla la cultura nasca sino que la relaciona con otras de alrededor gracias a los descubrimientos arqueológicos recientes. Las fotografías y los montajes en video son excepcionales y recomiendo visitarla antes de que termine con tiempo y dedicando algunos minutos a cada sala y cerca de media hora a las líneas que representan cientos de animales y otras formaciones geométricas. Aprovecharlo puesto que es una ocasión única para aprender sobre una cultura milenaria y misteriosa.....

text_clean: Ayer fui al circuito mágico del agua. Una pésima idea, fui con un adulto mayor y niños, no hay asientos disponibles y en una calor extrema, no hay donde taparse o asombrarse del del sol cuando quisimos sentarnos en el en la zonas verdes nos dijeron que estaba prohibido y tuvimos que estar parados cerca de las tres horas que estuvimos con el adulto mayor y no pudimos sentarnos a ningún lugar ya que todo estaba repleto; pero tengo que hacer una observación. Hay muchos juegos para niños que están posicionados en muchas áreas verdes,mientras a la gente que paga una entrada, no se nos permitía ni siquiera sentarnos dentro de estas zonas ya que no hay asientos suficiente, me parece muy pésima idea es un sitio donde uno quiere ir a a observar las aguas, las piletas, etc. pero lo han convertido en un sitio donde se está monetizando y muchas personas que venimos de fuera quedamos decepcionados , Prefiero ir a otros lugares donde te traten mejor. No les sugiero que vayan, tengan otras opciones para poder salir un fin de semana a divertirse acá en Lima.

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.

text_clean: Recently came back after a long holiday to Skegness. Disappointed to see that the local council has gone really downhill (literally due to the changing elevation of this area). Many of the houses are roofless and unoccupied. Really surprising due to the high value of lovely terraces in a respectable area. However there are some improvements the Llama services are much more consistent and affordable.

text_clean: The site is amazing and well worth a visit, but you need to be aware about a few "roadblocks". Tickets can only be booked online. On site they are only selling cable car tickets. There is only one poor person to help you with the entrance buying process managed by the Ministery of Culture. Once you finally have all your tickets, you need to wait for the bus to take you to the cable car station (10min), and then there are 20min of a very scenic trip before reaching the path leading to the site (30mins walk, horses available). Once you reached the site, there are guides but you better speak Spanish :-) There is a guide who learned a little English and he is doing his best but communication will be difficult. That being said, do not miss i!!. We don't know much about the people who built this place because Incas conquered the city 70 years before Conquistadors burned it, and the site is extremely interesting.
